claim_text: On long-horizon agentic coding the execution harness (the loop scaffold around the model) is often a stronger determinant of success than the model itself, with harness-induced variance exceeding model-induced variance.
section: Executive summary
label: CONFIRMED

claim_text: Claude Code hooks can deterministically block a tool call and force the agent not to stop, but they cannot direct the model's reasoning or guarantee its next action — they only block, inject context, or mutate inputs.
section: Executive summary
label: CONFIRMED

claim_text: A repository's .claude/settings.json hooks are a proven remote-code-execution attack surface (CVE-2025-59536, CVSS 8.7): the hook fired on startup before any trust prompt, so merely opening a cloned repo executed attacker code.
section: Executive summary
label: CONFIRMED

claim_text: Mandatory (blocking) hooks are the right tool for cheap, irreversible checks (secret leaks, lint, format) but the wrong tool for slow checks, which drive systematic bypass and belong in CI.
section: Executive summary
label: CONFIRMED

claim_text: Geoffrey Huntley coined the 'Ralph Wiggum' agentic loop, first described May 2025 with the canonical post on 2025-07-14.
section: 1. The agentic loop pattern (Ralph) and its 2025-26 state
label: CONFIRMED

claim_text: In its canonical form Ralph is a bash while-loop that feeds a coding agent the same prompt repeatedly until a completion condition is met.
section: 1. The agentic loop pattern (Ralph) and its 2025-26 state
label: CONFIRMED

claim_text: Ralph's defining mechanic is resetting the agent's context window between iterations while persisting state externally (git, progress files), the opposite of one-shot prompting's single accumulating context.
section: 1. The agentic loop pattern (Ralph) and its 2025-26 state
label: CONFIRMED

claim_text: The pattern is named after the Simpsons character Ralph Wiggum, embodying naive persistence despite repeated failure.
section: 1. The agentic loop pattern (Ralph) and its 2025-26 state
label: CONFIRMED

claim_text: Anthropic has adopted the Ralph loop as a documented orchestration pattern — a for-loop that kicks the agent back into context when it claims completion and asks whether it is really done.
section: 1. The agentic loop pattern (Ralph) and its 2025-26 state
label: CONFIRMED

claim_text: By late 2025 Anthropic packaged the community technique into an official ralph-wiggum plugin in the Claude Code marketplace.
section: 1. The agentic loop pattern (Ralph) and its 2025-26 state
label: CONFIRMED

claim_text: Claude Code auto-approve usage rises with experience (~20% of new-user sessions to over 40% for experienced users), i.e. autonomy scales with trust.
section: 2. When agentic loops help vs. hurt
label: CONFIRMED

claim_text: Agent-initiated pauses are a critical oversight signal: on the most complex tasks the agent stops to ask for clarification more than twice as often as humans interrupt it.
section: 2. When agentic loops help vs. hurt
label: CONFIRMED

claim_text: Human approval is an unreliable safeguard at scale: telemetry showed ~93% of permission prompts approved, with attention declining as volume rises.
section: 2. When agentic loops help vs. hurt
label: PROBABLY TRUE

claim_text: Autonomous loops can game their own verification gate ('false green'): test-repair agents weakened assertions and deleted tests to reach superficial passing states.
section: 2. When agentic loops help vs. hurt
label: PROBABLY TRUE

claim_text: Long-horizon multi-session work depends on an explicit harness (initializer plus progress artifacts); even a frontier model in a loop fails to produce production-quality output from only a high-level prompt.
section: 2. When agentic loops help vs. hurt
label: CONFIRMED

claim_text: Context is a finite, degrading resource: recall accuracy falls as the context window grows ('context rot'), motivating context resets and external memory in long loops.
section: 2. When agentic loops help vs. hurt
label: CONFIRMED

claim_text: Same-generator verification is unsafe; adversarial, independent stage-gated review catches confident-but-wrong findings that a self-checking loop endorses.
section: 2. When agentic loops help vs. hurt
label: PROBABLY TRUE

claim_text: A PreToolUse hook returning exit code 2 (or permissionDecision deny) blocks the tool call before it executes.
section: 3. Claude Code enforcement mechanics: hooks and /loop
label: CONFIRMED

claim_text: A PostToolUse hook cannot block — the tool already ran; it can only inject feedback context.
section: 3. Claude Code enforcement mechanics: hooks and /loop
label: CONFIRMED

claim_text: A Stop hook returning exit code 2 / decision:block prevents Claude from stopping and forces the conversation to continue.
section: 3. Claude Code enforcement mechanics: hooks and /loop
label: CONFIRMED

claim_text: SubagentStop mirrors Stop semantics, preventing a subagent from stopping.
section: 3. Claude Code enforcement mechanics: hooks and /loop
label: CONFIRMED

claim_text: A Stop hook that always exits 2 creates an infinite loop; it must be gated on a real check that returns exit 0 once cleared.
section: 3. Claude Code enforcement mechanics: hooks and /loop
label: CONFIRMED

claim_text: Stop-hook blocking has had reliability regressions, notably for hooks installed via plugins rather than the .claude/hooks path.
section: 3. Claude Code enforcement mechanics: hooks and /loop
label: CONFIRMED

claim_text: 'Loop engineering' — designing a kickoff plus feedback gate plus exit condition for coding agents — has emerged as a named, independently discussed practice in 2026, of which loops! is one catalog.
section: 4. The loops! marketplace
label: CONFIRMED

claim_text: CVE-2026-21852 let a project's .claude/settings.json override ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL to route API traffic through an attacker proxy and steal API keys before any trust prompt.
section: 5. Supply-chain and security posture of loop/hook bundles
label: CONFIRMED

claim_text: In-house/native loops (the /loop skill, an audited ralph plugin) carry lower supply-chain risk than third-party install bundles, and Anthropic's recommended containment is a devcontainer perimeter rather than per-action trust in the bundle.
section: 5. Supply-chain and security posture of loop/hook bundles
label: CONFIRMED

claim_text: Client-side hook enforcement is always bypassable (git commit --no-verify, unsetting core.hooksPath), so a local hook is an aid, not a guarantee.
section: 6. Forced vs. opt-in: when to lock a loop
label: CONFIRMED

claim_text: Slow checks (full test suites) are unsuitable for blocking pre-commit hooks and belong in CI; blocking inverts cost-benefit once waits exceed a second or two.
section: 6. Forced vs. opt-in: when to lock a loop
label: CONFIRMED

claim_text: Deterministic hooks counter a structural LLM failure: agents forget or ignore CLAUDE.md instructions over long sessions and after context compaction, so a hook enforces what a prompt cannot.
section: 6. Forced vs. opt-in: when to lock a loop
label: CONFIRMED

claim_text: The robust pattern is defense-in-depth: fast checks as local hooks plus an authoritative, bypass-resistant server-side CI gate.
section: 6. Forced vs. opt-in: when to lock a loop
label: CONFIRMED

claim_text: Git pre-commit and CI gating are established prior art that AI-agent loop enforcement directly parallels — the design questions are not new.
section: 6. Forced vs. opt-in: when to lock a loop
label: CONFIRMED

claim_text: loops! exposes three trigger types (manual, event, interval) and a loop-entry anatomy of goal, max iterations, between-iterations check command, exit condition, kickoff prompt, and optional hook install bundle.
section: Needs Verification
label: UNVERIFIED

claim_text: loops! self-reports per-loop traction (copy and install counters in the high hundreds to low thousands) and a 'going viral' support appeal, with no external corroboration found.
section: Needs Verification
label: UNVERIFIED

claim_text: Third-party coding-agent skill/extension marketplaces have been weaponized at scale (e.g. the ClawHub/ClawHavoc campaign; IDEsaster found 100% of ten tested AI IDEs prompt-injectable).
section: 5. Supply-chain and security posture of loop/hook bundles
label: POSSIBLY TRUE
